name: Aelyn Kantoro
whyHere: You were sent, or you were seeded, or you simply wanted to see something finish inside a single lifetime.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

turns.summary: Aelyn arrives at the Heartroot at dawn — a stranger in the grown city, Branch wide with recognition, the river's wrongness still in her gut.
turns.narration:
The Heartroot receives you the way old things receive all things — without urgency. You came through the outer gate an hour before sunrise, following a root-road that the city had grown into the forest floor over what must have been centuries, and now you stand in a hall that is also, unmistakably, a tree. The ceiling arches thirty feet above in living wood, breathing visibly in the early light — a slow expansion and contraction you first thought was your own tired eyes, until you touched the bark and felt it under your palm. The floor is soft with moss that has been here longer than most nations. Morning light comes in through gaps the wood has grown specifically to admit it: oval, warm, purposeful.

Branch stands beside your left knee, roots finding purchase in the moss with the soft sound of paper tearing. It has been doing that the whole walk here — a quiet, recurring grip-and-release, as if the road itself needed reassurance. Now, in this hall, it stills completely. The buds at its shoulders open, one by one, four small pale petals each. That is how you have come to read it: stillness and open buds mean something close to wonder.

"Root," it says, at last. One word, offered carefully, the way it offers all new words. It means: I recognize this. I am home in a way I did not know I missed.

You are not home. You came from somewhere much quieter — your mother's forest, the hidden refuge where the trees are old and the air is clean and everything you ever knew is ending slowly, has been ending, is now over. She died ten days ago. You packed your Traveler's Pack, took your waterskin from the spring while it still ran clear, and walked out of the only world you had ever known. The outside found you before you found it: the Echo River where you forded it yesterday ran gray-green in the early light, wrong in a way that sat in your stomach all day. You had heard the patrol reports. Seeing it is different.

The hall is not empty. Three Rootkin move through it at the slow, deliberate pace of people who have nowhere to be in the next hour and would like it that way. One tends a sapling in a planter — the sapling is, you realize after a moment, looking back at you. Not the incurious animal gaze of a plant. The direct, assessing gaze of a child.

turns.player: Open your senses with Lifesense — take the quiet moment to feel the hall and whatever living things move through its walls before anyone notices you doing it.
turns.summary: Aelyn reads the hall with Lifesense — five living signatures, the city's walls holding something that isn't quite life, and a sapling-child that waves hello.
turns.narration:
The reach comes quietly — you let it out the way you'd open a hand, not a door. Lifesense moves through the hall in a slow pulse, and the world returns in warmth-shapes: the tender near the planter, deliberate and unhurried, a steady ember of vitality. The two at the far end, similarly calm, similarly slow — these are not people in a hurry and their bodies know it. The sapling in the planter reads bright, almost startlingly so, a small fierce heat compared to the even glow of the adult Rootkin around it.

The walls themselves — that's where the partial slips. You feel *something* in the wood, distributed and faint, a warmth that isn't quite alive in any way you have a word for, and isn't quite not. It moves in the grain, slow as sap. You can't hold it long enough to understand it before it diffuses and you lose the thread.

Branch, beside your knee, has gone very still. Its buds are wide open. When you glance down, it is looking at the sapling in the planter with an attention that is, for something that moves like weather, almost urgent.

The sapling looks back at you. Then, with the deliberateness of something that has thought carefully about the action, it raises one thin branch in a gesture that is almost certainly a greeting.

turns.player: Crouch down to Branch's level and ask quietly what it sees — let it speak first.
turns.summary: Branch speaks its first real words in the Heartroot — 'old, very old, and watching' — as the sapling-child waits for acknowledgment.
turns.narration:
You lower yourself to one knee on the moss, bringing your face level with Branch's shoulder-height crown. Up close, the buds are fully splayed — five of them, stiff and wide-open, the posture it takes when something has caught all of its attention at once. You've seen it fix on a thriving oak with that same still hunger, and on a dying one with something closer to grief. This is neither.

"Branch," you say quietly, your voice barely above the sound the bark makes in the morning — that slow, pressured exhale. "What do you see?"

A long pause. The kind that would be uncomfortable with anyone else. Then, in the slow creak that is its voice, one word at a time: "Old." Another pause, longer. "Very. Old. And." The buds quiver slightly. "Watching."

Across the room, the sapling-child still holds its thin branch extended in your direction — a greeting that has been waiting, patient and unhurried, for however long a three-year-old grown-hall person considers reasonable. The tender near the planter has stopped adjusting the soil entirely. She does not look at you directly, but her hands are still, and the quiet way she's not-looking is its own kind of attention.

turns.player: Use The Speaking Grove — ask the sapling what it is watching, in the language the green understands.
turns.summary: Aelyn speaks to the sapling through the Grove — learns the watching thing predates the hall itself, grown around rather than built in.
turns.narration:
You settle your weight fully onto one knee and let the ability come — not pushed, just opened, the way you've learned to speak to the old birches back home. The Speaking Grove rises in your chest like a breath held too long finally let go, and you reach toward the sapling in the language that doesn't use words: a question laid flat and open, patient, the way good soil waits for rain.

The sapling's raised branch trembles once. Then it answers.

Not in images exactly — more like a pressure, a direction. Something has been here longer than the hall has. Longer than the Rootkin who grew the hall. The sapling isn't afraid of it. It is simply reporting, the way a very young thing reports anything it has sensed: here, here, here. The watching is not a person. It is not the walls, exactly. It is something that the walls grew around, the way bark grows around a wound, and the sapling has known it since it was a seed, and considers this ordinary.

Branch has gone very still beside you. One of its roots has pressed flat against the moss floor, and you recognize that posture — it does it when it is listening to something you cannot hear yet. The tender near the planter has still not moved, but her head has tilted by a fraction, and you have the distinct sense she has been watching you work the whole time and is simply deciding what she thinks of it.

turns.player: Acknowledge the sapling's greeting properly — you've been listening but haven't said hello. Meet it where it is.
turns.summary: Aelyn greets the sapling properly; the tender's attention sharpens with quiet decision.
turns.narration:
You turn fully toward the sapling and do what you should have done from the start — you acknowledge it. Not with words. You press two fingers to the moss at the edge of its planter, an old gesture from home, the kind your mother used when she wanted a tree to know it had been seen. Then you lift your chin just slightly in its direction, the way you'd greet a person across a crowded room.

The sapling's raised branch dips once, slow and deliberate, and then rises again. The small fierce warmth you felt through Lifesense brightens for a moment — pleased, maybe, or simply more attentive. One of its smaller roots shifts against the soil in the planter, resettling, the way a person shifts their weight when they've been standing still too long and someone has finally noticed.

Branch makes a sound — not a word, just a low creak of what you've come to recognize as satisfaction. Its buds stay fully open but the rigidity in them eases a fraction. It was waiting for you to do that.

The tender by the planter has not moved, but the angle of her attention has. She is watching you both now — you, Branch, and the sapling — with the particular stillness of someone who has made a decision about something and is waiting to see if they were right.

turns.player: Ask the sapling directly — reach through the Grove once more, and ask where the watching thing is centered within the hall.
turns.summary: The sapling gave Aelyn a direction — down, into the hall's deepest roots — and the tender spoke for the first time.
turns.narration:
You settle the request into the Grove again — the same patient open-handedness, the same held breath. The sapling receives it without hesitation this time. You asked a direction, and it gives you one.

A pressure builds in your left shoulder. Not pain — more like the way you feel a fire's warmth on one side of your face when the other is in cool air. The sapling's branch tilts fractionally, and through the Grove you get something clear enough to be almost spatial: inward. Deeper. Below the entrance hall, below the planter, down through the root-floor and the old soil beneath, where the Heartroot's deepest grain runs. Not a room. Not a chamber. A center — the way a seed has a center before anything grows from it.

You open your eyes. The hall looks unchanged — bark ceiling exhaling slow, moss underfoot, the two distant Rootkin still moving unhurried at the far end. But you find yourself looking at the floor differently, at the seam where the planter's roots disappear into the ground, and you know the direction now even if you don't know what's at the end of it.

Branch makes a soft sound, a low single creak — not a word, just acknowledgment. It felt the question go through. Beside you, the tender takes one deliberate step closer, unhurried, and speaks for the first time. Her voice is the kind that has never been in a hurry about anything. "You speak to the young one." It is not a question.

turns.player: Ask her what she decided just now — she's been watching and making up her mind since I arrived; I want to know what she concluded.
turns.summary: The tender reveals her decision: Aelyn is not most visitors — and asks who she is and what the sapling said.
turns.narration:
The tender doesn't look surprised that you noticed. She holds your gaze for a moment — unhurried, the way the Heartroot itself is unhurried — and then glances at Branch, who is still creaking softly near the planter's edge, buds open.

'I watched you come in,' she says. Her voice is low, measured, the cadence of someone used to taking weeks to finish a thought. 'You didn't announce yourself. You didn't touch anything you weren't invited to touch. And you stopped — here — and listened first.' A pause, long enough that it doesn't feel like hesitation. 'Most visitors go straight to the far hall and ask about the age of the ceiling. I decided: you are not most visitors.'

She looks back at you, and something in her expression settles — not warmth exactly, but willingness. The kind you earn from someone who gives it rarely. 'I also decided,' she continues, 'that you were speaking to the young one through the Green. Not to it. Through it.' Her eyes drop briefly to the planter, to Branch's open buds, to your two fingers still resting at the moss edge. 'That is not a thing most people know how to do, and fewer still bother.' She tilts her head, measuring. 'So. Who are you, and what did it tell you?'

turns.player: Give your name and where you're from — but hold back what the sapling told you. That feels like yours to carry a little longer.
turns.summary: Aelyn gives her name and origin; the tender reveals hers — Maren — and notices Branch.
turns.narration:
You give her your name — Aelyn Kantoro — and watch it settle into the space between you the way names do here, slowly, with weight. Then you tell her the shorter truth: you came from the forest. The hidden one, to the east, where the trees are old enough to remember the valley before the Transition. You don't name the refuge. You don't explain your mother, or Branch, or the way you felt the hall breathing before you'd taken three steps inside. Those are yours.

What the sapling told you — that stays with you too. Not from distrust. From something more like care. You're not sure the tender needs to hear it from you when she could ask the sapling herself. And you're not sure yet what it means to carry that knowledge outward.

The tender receives your name without repeating it, which feels correct. She lets the silence after it stand for a moment — not empty, just full of something else. Then she gives you hers, with the same unhurried plainness: 'Maren.' She inclines her head a fraction, and the gesture carries the same weight as a handshake would, somewhere else. Branch gives one slow, satisfied creak from the planter's edge.

'The forest to the east,' Maren says, not quite a question. Her eyes move to Branch — to the small Rootkin child still listening with all five buds open — and something in her expression shifts. Not surprise. More like a piece of something fitting into a place she'd been leaving open. She looks back at you. 'And you brought one of ours with you.'

turns.player: Ask Maren about the thing below — not what the sapling told you, but whether the Heartroot has a name for what lives in its deepest grain.
turns.summary: Maren names the presence below — the Root-That-Remembers — and turns the question back on Aelyn.
turns.narration:
Maren has your name now, and Branch's presence registered in her eyes — a quick, private recognition she hasn't named yet. You let the quiet settle for one more breath before you ask.

'The Heartroot grew around something,' you say, keeping your voice as level as hers. 'Something older than the hall. Does the city have a name for what lives in its deepest grain?'

Maren is quiet long enough that you hear Branch shift its roots on the moss — one soft creak, unhurried. Then she exhales slowly and looks at the floor, or maybe through it, the way you look at a body of water when you're thinking about depth.

'We call it the Root-That-Remembers,' she says. 'Or some of us do. Others say you shouldn't call it anything, because naming it implies you understand it, and we don't.' She looks up at you. 'It was here before the first Rootkin put the first seed in this ground. We grew the hall around it because it seemed — right. The way you build a house around a good hearth.' A pause. 'It hasn't spoken to anyone alive. Not in any way we recognize as speech.' Her eyes flick briefly to the planter — to the sapling, then to Branch — and back to you. Something careful enters her expression. 'Why do you ask?'

turns.player: Use Lifesense — reach down through the floor toward whatever is below and feel whether the Root-That-Remembers is aware of this conversation right now.
turns.summary: Lifesense confirms the Root-That-Remembers is aware — distributed, slow, attending.
turns.narration:
You press two fingers a little harder into the moss and let Lifesense go downward — not out across the hall, but *down*, the way you'd lower a hand into dark water to feel for a current. The familiar warmth of the ability moves through you, and you send it into the floor.

What comes back is not nothing.

It is not the crisp warmth of a body, not the close heat of the sapling above you. It is something slower — distributed through the root-floor the way heat moves through stone left all day in sun. Settled. Occupying. And it is not simply *there*. There is an orientation to it, a toward-ness that you've never felt in a living thing before: diffuse attention, like the moment before a very old tree decides to drop a branch. The Root-That-Remembers is not asleep. It registers this conversation the way a deep lake registers a stone dropped at its surface — not ignoring it, not answering it, simply *aware* that something has touched the air above.

You lift your fingers from the moss slowly. Maren is still watching. Branch has gone still at the planter's edge, all five buds tipped downward, as if it too felt something move through the floor.

turns.player: Say nothing to Maren yet — sit with what you felt and reach through the Grove to Branch quietly, to see if the companion felt the same attention moving through the floor.
turns.summary: Aelyn reaches through the Grove to Branch — the companion felt the Root-That-Remembers attending too, its buds turned down in deliberate stillness.
turns.narration:
You don't answer Maren's question yet. You let it rest there between you, unanswered but not refused, and turn your attention inward — or rather, downward, and sideways, toward Branch.

The Grove opens quietly, the way it does when you're not asking for much. Not a request this time. More like pressing your hand flat against a door to feel whether there's warmth on the other side. You reach toward Branch through the green — through the moss underfoot and the roots threaded beneath it — and what comes back is immediate and small: the companion's awareness, close and present, like a hand already extended in your direction. It felt it too. Not the same way you did — Branch doesn't have Lifesense, doesn't read vigor and warmth the way you do — but it is standing at the planter's edge with all five buds tipped down toward the floor, and through the Grove you sense something that in a person you'd call stillness-on-purpose. The kind that means you are listening very hard.

Branch doesn't look up. But one bud opens, just partway, facing you.

Maren hasn't moved. She is patient in the way the hall itself is patient — not waiting for you to hurry, just present, her question still in the air. The sapling beside Branch keeps its branch raised. The light through the oval gaps in the bark ceiling has shifted a little, morning pulling further in, and somewhere at the far end of the hall the two other Rootkin have gone quiet too, though they haven't turned.

turns.player: Say nothing more to Maren yet — let Branch speak if it wants to. It felt what you felt; it may have something to say that you don't.
turns.summary: Branch speaks to Maren directly; the Root-That-Remembers has made itself known to a visitor for the first time in a long time.
turns.narration: The silence you offer is deliberate — not evasion, just room. Branch speaks: 'Down. Old. Watching. Us.' Maren settles, names what she sees — your cutting felt it, and so did you — and asks what the Root-That-Remembers felt like from your side of the floor.

turns.player: Answer, but ask in return: has anyone tried to reach it deliberately — to go down where the oldest roots run?
turns.summary: Aelyn describes what the Root-That-Remembers felt like; Maren reveals that the old ones go down to listen, but no one has ever gone to be heard.
turns.narration:
You let the silence settle for one more breath, feeling the warmth still fading from your fingertips where the moss held it. Then you answer.

"Like heat in stone," you say. "Not a body — nothing that crisp. Distributed, the way warmth spreads through something solid that's been sitting in sun for a very long time. But it wasn't still. There was a toward-ness to it." You press your fingers into the moss once more, a gesture more than an action. "It registered us. Not just me — us. The conversation, Branch, all of it." You glance up at Maren. "I've felt living things through walls and I've felt the barely-alive. This was neither. It was attending."

Maren is quiet in the way that means she is weighing the words against something she already knows. Her eyes move briefly to Branch — whose single bud is still aimed in her direction — and then back to you.

"That matches," she says, and nothing more for a moment.

Then you ask your own question, plain and direct: has anyone tried to reach it deliberately? To go down where the oldest roots run, and meet it?

Something shifts in Maren's expression — not alarm, not quite reluctance. The quality of a thing being considered that has not been considered for some time. "The Heartroot grows around it," she says slowly, "the way you'd grow a city around a well. We tend the distance." A pause. "Some of the older Rootkin have walked the deep grain. Not many. Not in my memory, and my memory is not short." She looks at you with that unhurried directness. "No one has gone down asking to be heard by it. They go down to listen. Those are not the same thing."

turns.player: Tell Maren what the sapling gave you — the direction inward, below — and ask whether that matches where the deep grain actually runs.
turns.summary: Aelyn shared the sapling's direction with Maren; the deep grain runs exactly where the sapling pointed — below the entrance hall, from before the Rootkin were.
turns.narration:
You decide the direction is no longer yours alone to carry. 'The sapling told me something,' you say. 'When I asked where the presence was centered — it gave me a direction. Inward. Below. Down through the root-floor into the oldest grain.' You watch Maren's face as you say it.

She doesn't look surprised. She looks, if anything, like someone who has just confirmed the spelling of a word she already knew how to say. 'Yes,' she says simply. 'That is where they go.' A pause — not hesitation, just her rhythm. 'The deep grain runs beneath the whole entrance hall. It is why we planted the first hall here, above this spot. Not to be near it. To be worthy of it.' Her gaze moves briefly to the floor, the way you'd glance at something load-bearing. 'It runs down further than we have ever fully mapped. Old roots, from before this city was a thought in anyone's mind. From before us.'

Branch makes a small sound — a single, slow creak, like a floorboard settling in winter. One bud tips toward the floor. Just that.

Maren looks at you steadily. 'The sapling gave you the direction,' she says, and there is nothing accusatory in it — only weight. 'Which means it trusted you with something it could have kept. And the Root-That-Remembers attended while it did.' The two distant Rootkin at the far end of the hall have not moved, but you feel the quality of their stillness change — more listening, less waiting. Maren's voice stays level. 'Tell me, Aelyn Kantoro — what do you want to do with that?'

turns.player: Use Lifesense again — but this time reach not just toward the presence, but alongside it. Feel whether the Root-That-Remembers is still attending in the same way, or whether the sapling's disclosure changed something in its orientation.
turns.summary: Lifesense finds the Root-That-Remembers has narrowed its attention from the room to Aelyn specifically.
turns.narration:
You press both palms flat against the moss this time, not just fingertips. The Lifesense opens differently than it did before — less like casting a line down and more like leaning against something that is already leaning back.

The Root-That-Remembers is still there. But the attending has shifted. Before, it felt like warmth diffusing toward a sound — curious, oriented, general. Now it has a quality you don't have a clean word for: the way a person stops scanning a room because they have found the face they were looking for. The warmth below hasn't intensified. It has narrowed. It is not listening to the conversation anymore. It is listening to you.

Branch makes a small sound — a dry creak, like a door that has been left in the sun. One bud tilts upward.

You surface from the sense slowly, the moss cold under your palms where the morning hasn't reached yet. Maren has not moved. She is watching you the way she watches everything — as though she has all the time the city has, and she does.

turns.player: Stay quiet and turn your attention inward — not to the Root-That-Remembers, but to yourself. The narrowing of its focus raises a question: what does it recognize in you that it is listening for?
turns.summary: Aelyn turns Lifesense inward and recognizes a frequency in herself that predates her craft — the register the Root-That-Remembers is listening for.
turns.narration:
You don't reach outward this time. You let the Lifesense fold back on itself — not a cast line but a held breath — and turn it the only direction left: inward.

What you find is not a neat answer. It is more like catching yourself in a mirror at an odd angle and recognizing the shape before you recognize the face. There is something in you that hums faintly at the same register as the warmth below. Not loud. Not structured. More like the frequency a tuning fork remembers after the sound has stopped. You have felt it before in small moments — when you climbed the high beeches at home and the bark seemed to receive your weight gladly, when Branch first moved toward you before you called it, when you spoke into the sapling's quiet just an hour ago and the word landed without resistance. You assumed it was the Grove. The Grove is part of it. But the groove is older than the ability. It runs somewhere deeper in you, and you have no name for it yet.

What the Root-That-Remembers recognizes, you think, is not your craft. It is the register underneath your craft — the frequency that was there before you had a word for what you could do. Whether that register came from your mother's world or from something else you were born carrying, you cannot say. You only know that you have always had it, and until this moment you did not realize that something very old and very large might be able to hear it.

You lift your palms from the moss slowly. Maren is still watching you. Branch has one bud tipped fully open. The entrance hall holds its breath.

turns.player: Tell Maren what you just found — that it isn't the Grove it hears. Something older. Ask whether she has heard the Rootkin speak of a person carrying a different kind of root.
turns.summary: Aelyn names what she found in herself — not the Grove but something older — and Maren gives it a word: the deep-woven, a union of green and something more ancient, thought to be legend.
turns.narration:
You open your eyes and find Maren still watching — patient as bark, giving you the full weight of her attention without pressing for it. 'It isn't the Grove,' you say, and your voice comes out quieter than you intended. 'It heard me before I used the Grove. Whatever it is listening for — it's older than my craft. It runs under all of it.' You press your palms once more against the moss, feeling that hum still vibrating below — faint, recognizable now in a way it wasn't an hour ago. 'Has anyone among the Rootkin spoken of a person carrying something like that? Not a Grove-speaker. Something underneath. A different kind of root.'

Maren is still for a long moment. The two distant Rootkin have not moved, but the quality of their stillness changes — from listening to holding. She reaches out and presses her own palm flat against the planter's edge, the way you might lay a hand on a door to feel who is on the other side. 'There is a word,' she says, and her voice carries the careful weight of someone lifting something fragile. 'We do not use it often. The deep-woven. A person in whom the green and something older run together — not two separate things learned, but one thing undivided at the source.' She lifts her hand from the wood. 'We thought it was a story from before the city was planted. The kind of story that is true in the way old stories are true — meaning: it happened, but not to anyone living.'

Branch makes a sound — a slow, considered creak, one bud turning toward you. Maren's gaze follows it, then comes back to your face with something that is not quite disbelief but closer to wonder held carefully in both hands. 'You were born in the hidden places,' she says. It isn't a question. She doesn't ask how she knows — the city told her, or the floor did, or she has simply been watching long enough. 'What your father is — I will not ask yet. But the deep grain recognized something in you before you reached for it.' Her chin tips once, a small decisive motion. 'That is not the Grove. That is older than the Grove. And it is not nothing.'

turns.player: Tell her plainly: your father is not of the green. Ask whether a person whose roots run in two completely different worlds — one of nature, one of something else — could still be deep-woven.
turns.summary: Aelyn told Maren her father is not of the green — Maren's certainty about deep-woven cracked open, and the Root-That-Remembers is unchanged in its recognition.
turns.narration:
You lift your hands from the moss and hold Maren's gaze. 'There is something else you should know,' you say. 'My father — he is not of the green. Not at all.' You let that sit for a breath. The morning light shifts through the oval gaps above, laying slow bars across the bark floor. 'My mother carried the green. He carried something different — something built, not grown. And I came from both of them.' You watch her face as you say it, reading the small adjustments there — not alarm, not recoil, something more careful than either. 'Can a person still be deep-woven if their roots run into two completely different worlds? One of nature — and one that isn't?'

Maren does not answer immediately. She turns away from you slightly, the way someone does when the question requires more than one room of their mind. Branch makes a slow creak — not a word, just presence — and its bud-tips open a fraction, all five, the way they do when it is holding something it doesn't yet have the words for. The sapling in the planter dips its highest branch, almost imperceptibly, toward the floor below.

When Maren turns back her expression has the quality of someone who has just placed a heavy thing in a new spot and is now deciding whether the floor can hold it. 'The deep-woven we named in legend,' she says slowly, 'were of the green at the source — we assumed one root only, going down into old earth.' She pauses. 'We may have assumed too much.' She studies you with a directness that is not accusatory — she is simply measuring. 'What the Root-That-Remembers hears in you — it did not wait for us to describe your father. It recognized you before you spoke a word to it.' Another pause. 'That is not nothing.'

At the far end of the hall, one of the two distant Rootkin takes a slow step closer. Not intrusive — the way a tree leans toward water, a little at a time, because it cannot help it. The morning has shifted from early to full, the light-gaps brightening across the moss floor.

turns.player: Press your palms to the moss again and let Lifesense reach down — not to the Root-That-Remembers, but toward the exact point where its attention is narrowed on you. See if the recognition runs both ways now.
turns.summary: Lifesense traced the Root-That-Remembers' attention to its source and found mutual recognition — Aelyn has been expected.
turns.narration:
Your palms settle against the moss and the Lifesense opens like a door that has been waiting. You reach down — not broadly, not casting a wide field — but precisely, following the narrow line of the Root-That-Remembers' attention the way you would follow a taut wire to its anchor. Down through the bark floor, through the root-web, through layers of compressed time in the grain.

And then something answers.

Not words. Not even sensation in the ordinary sense. It is more like recognition — mutual, simultaneous, the way two people look up at the same sound at the same instant and know they both heard it. The Root-That-Remembers has been attending you. But you understand now, with a certainty that arrives whole and complete, that it has been doing something more than observing. It has been waiting. Not for a Rootkin. Not for a Grove-speaker. The frequency in you that you found minutes ago — the older hum beneath your craft — it is not simply something the Root recognizes. It is something the Root knows. Knows the way a tree knows the specific water-table it has grown into over a century: intimately, by its absence and presence both, by every small change in the reaching.

The warmth below you shifts. Not closer. It doesn't move. But its quality changes — the way a face changes when recognition stops being effort and becomes ease. You feel, unmistakably, that you have been expected. Not today. Not even in this lifetime. But that your arrival here, this conversation, these specific hands on this specific moss, completes something that has been open for a very long time. The deep grain hums once, clean and low, and then holds steady — a held breath, a question left deliberately unanswered, an invitation shaped like a door left ajar.

You surface slowly. Branch's five buds are fully open and not moving. Maren has not spoken, but her eyes are no longer measuring — she is simply watching, the way you watch something you know you will carry for a long time.

turns.player: Tell Maren what you just felt — not the hum, but the specific quality of it: that you were expected. Ask what the Rootkin know of something waiting beneath the city that predates them.
turns.summary: Aelyn names the recognition plainly — she was expected — and Maren reveals what the Rootkin know: the Root-That-Remembers predates them, and its name comes not from understanding but from the plain fact of its long attention.
turns.narration:
You lift your palms from the moss and sit back on your heels. The morning light has shifted, the oval gaps above carrying it a little further across the floor now.

'It recognized me,' you say to Maren. Not as a question, not with particular weight — just the plain fact of it. 'Not the way you recognize a stranger who does something interesting. The way you recognize something you have been waiting for, for a long time.' You look at your hands, still faintly warm from the contact. 'I was expected. Whatever is down there — it was not surprised by me at all.'

Branch has not moved. All five buds still open and perfectly still, which on Branch means it is holding something with both hands.

Maren exhales through her nose — a slow, considering sound. 'The Rootkin know it exists,' she says at last. 'We do not speak of it often, because speaking of it did not seem to change anything, and Rootkin are not given to words that change nothing.' She folds her hands together at her waist, the way someone does when they are choosing what comes next. 'Before the first hall was planted — before any of us were here — something had already chosen this soil. The roots we put down met roots already there, going deeper than we have ever followed. We have called it different things in different ages. The name that has lasted is the Root-That-Remembers.' A pause. 'We did not name it because we understand what it remembers. We named it because it was clearly doing so.'

The closer of the two distant Rootkin takes a slow step — not toward you, but not away. Listening, plainly, and making no pretense otherwise. Maren glances toward them with the mild, unhurried look of someone who finds that entirely appropriate.

turns.player: Ask Maren plainly what comes next. You were expected — the Root-That-Remembers has been waiting. Does it want something? Is there a way to answer it?
turns.summary: Maren revealed there is a passage into the deep grain — grown four hundred years ago, never answered — and Branch spoke a single word: Down.
turns.narration:
You ask it plainly, the way you have asked everything this morning — without ceremony, because ceremony feels dishonest here. 'It was waiting for me. What does it want? Is there a way to answer it?'

Maren is quiet for a moment. Not the silence of someone who doesn't know — the silence of someone choosing which words to use. She unfolds her hands and lets them rest at her sides, and the small shift in her posture is the most open you have seen her. 'We do not know what it wants,' she says at last. 'That is the honest answer. It has been attended below this hall for longer than we have had words for it, and it has never acted. Never moved upward. Never spoken through the green in any way we could hear.' A breath. 'Until today, we had never heard of anyone it recognized.'

Branch makes a sound — low, a slow creak from deep in its grain — and then, with the careful precision of a word it has been holding for exactly this moment: 'Down.'

The word sits in the hall like a stone set in moss. The closer of the two distant Rootkin has stopped pretending to be doing anything else at all. The sapling-child's highest branch is still dipped toward the floor, patient as water finding its level.

Maren looks at Branch, then at you. 'There is a way into the deep grain,' she says. 'A passage the Rootkin grew over four hundred years ago, when we first understood what lay beneath. We grew it to be able to reach the roots — to witness, not to disturb.' She pauses. 'No one has walked it and heard anything back. We assumed it was not listening.' The quiet held between you says plainly what she does not: the assumption was wrong. 'I will not tell you to go. I will tell you the passage is there.'

turns.player: Ask Maren to show you the passage. You were expected — you intend to answer.
turns.summary: Maren leads Aelyn to the deep grain passage — four hundred years old, grown into the living floor, never answered.
turns.narration:
You stand. Not ceremoniously — just a simple rising from your heels, brushing moss from your palms. 'Show me the passage,' you say to Maren. 'I was expected. I intend to answer.'

Maren holds your gaze for a moment. Then she nods — a single, unhurried dip of her head — and turns toward the far end of the entrance hall without fanfare, as though she has been waiting for exactly these words for some time. The two distant Rootkin part fractionally, the closer one stepping back to clear a narrow path between the planters. Nobody speaks. Branch's buds are still open as it pivots on its roots and follows, the soft grip-release of its root-feet quiet against the moss.

Maren leads you past the great planter, past a stretch of wall where the bark has grown so dense it swallowed a doorway-shape whole, and to a place where the floor slopes almost imperceptibly downward. Here the moss thickens. Here the morning light from the oval gaps above does not quite reach. Carved into the living floor — or rather, grown there, coaxed open over years until the grain itself parted — is an opening barely wide enough for one person: a descending corridor of pale heartwood, the walls still faintly warm. Old roots lace across the threshold like a hand held out.

'Four hundred years,' Maren says quietly, looking down at it rather than at you. 'We grew it in three generations of patient asking. Whoever walked down came back changed — quieter, more inward — but they never said they heard anything back.' She steps aside. The passage exhales a breath of cool, damp air that smells like the oldest part of a forest: soil and slow water and something green that has never seen light. Branch stops at your left shoulder. Its single word from earlier hangs in the air like a direction. Down.

turns.player: Pause at the threshold and open Lifesense downward before you step — read what waits in the passage itself before committing.
turns.summary: Lifesense read the deep grain passage — old, alive, nothing hostile, the Root-That-Remembers waiting below with total certainty.
turns.narration:
You stop at the edge. One foot still on the moss, one hovering above the first root that laces across the threshold like a held-out hand. You close your eyes and let Lifesense go down.

It finds the passage immediately — and the passage is alive. Not in the way the entrance hall is alive, with its layered hum of bark and breathing wood and the slow pulse of the Rootkin moving through distant rooms. This is older. The roots that frame the corridor are enormous, pale things, and their life-pulse runs slow and deep, like a heartbeat measured in seasons rather than seconds. They are not sleeping. You feel them the way you feel something that has been very still for a very long time, waiting with the particular patience of something that has no reason to hurry. Three meters down, five — the passage narrows and then the roots open into something larger, and there your Lifesense finds the edge of the Root-That-Remembers properly: not a presence, not a node, but a field. Distributed and vast and absolutely unhurried. The same recognition as before. The same quality of long waiting. Nothing in the passage itself will stop you. Nothing there is hostile. What waits below is neither welcome nor warning — it is simply attention, turned fully upward, held in the precise stillness of something that has already decided you will come.

You open your eyes. The roots across the threshold are still. Branch has not moved. You are aware of Maren at your left, and the thin morning quiet behind you, and the passage descending into warmth and pale root-light ahead.

turns.player: Reach one hand back to Branch before you step — its roots, its age, the connection. Whatever is below, you don't go entirely alone.
turns.summary: Aelyn reaches back to Branch, and together they step across the threshold into the deep grain passage.
turns.narration:
You reach back without looking. Your hand finds Branch at shoulder height — not bark exactly, more like the first inch beneath bark, warm and faintly yielding, the way green wood feels when it's just decided to be a tree. Its roots stop their restless grip-release against the moss. All five buds are open. It does not pull you back or push you forward. It simply holds.

Maren watches this and says nothing. Whatever she sees in it, she keeps. The passage waits ahead of you, its pale heartwood walls slow-lit by the deep root-pulse below — a light with no obvious source, amber and faint, the color of something that has been patient a very long time.

You step across the threshold. The moss gives way to old root beneath your boot, and the temperature shifts — not cold, warmer, the close warmth of deep soil in early spring. The passage is narrow enough that you can touch both walls without fully extending your arms. The roots framing it are enormous up close, their surfaces smooth with age, and against your Lifesense they read like a slow tide: entirely present, entirely unconcerned with urgency. Something much older than the hall above attends from below.

Branch follows, its root-steps finding the corridor floor with careful deliberateness. Behind you, Maren does not descend — her feet stay at the threshold. You hear her exhale once, quietly. Then the passage takes you, and the entrance hall recedes into amber and root-lit dark.

turns.player: Speak into the passage as you descend — not loudly, but clearly. Let the Root-That-Remembers hear your voice before you arrive. Answer its waiting with the plain fact of your presence.
turns.summary: Aelyn speaks her presence into the deep grain passage — the Root-That-Remembers hears, and the pulse in the walls answers.
turns.narration:
Your voice comes out steadier than you expected. Not loud — the passage doesn't invite loudness. The words settle into the amber dark the way a stone settles into still water: 'I'm here. I heard you. I'm coming down.'

The roots respond. Not instantly, and not with anything you could call movement — but the slow pulse in the walls deepens by a beat, the way breathing deepens when something sleeping hears its name. The amber light below doesn't brighten, exactly. It sharpens. The air against your face is warmer than the air behind you, and carries the smell of very old earth — not rot, not soil exactly, but the deep mineral something that exists only where roots have been drinking the same water for centuries. Your voice is still moving through the grain, you realize. Still going down.

Branch makes a sound beside you — a soft creak, low and held. Not alarm. Recognition, maybe. Its buds have not closed since the threshold. It takes one root-step forward and then another, deliberate, matching your pace. Whatever the Root-That-Remembers is doing with the pulse in the walls, Branch feels it too.

Above you, framed in the threshold's pale heartwood, Maren has not moved. Her silhouette is still, arms at her sides, watching the two of you descend into the grain that predates everything she knows. You do not feel alone. The passage has heard you, and it is waiting — not with the patience of something that doubts you'll arrive, but with the patience of something that already knew you would.

turns.player: Speak again as you descend — not to announce yourself this time, but to ask a plain question: what do you want me to know?
turns.summary: Aelyn asks what the Root-That-Remembers wants her to know — a partial answer arrives: old, many, and her as a point of convergence long traced. The descent continues.
turns.narration:
You keep your feet moving — one careful step at a time down the root lattice, Branch just behind your left shoulder — and you speak again into the amber dark. Still not loud. You say it the way you'd say something to someone in the same room who already knew you were there: 'What do you want me to know?'

The passage does not answer with words. You did not expect words. What comes back is texture — not sound, not vision, something closer to pressure against the inside of your chest, the way deep water presses when you dive past a certain depth. Images aren't right either. It is more like: old. Then: many. Then something that has no clean translation, a sense of roots threading through strata of rock and time, of a network that has been listening outward through the soil in every direction for longer than the Rootkin have existed, and of you — specifically you — as a point of convergence it has been tracing back through those threads for a very long time.

Not all of it arrives at once. Part of it cuts off, like a sentence interrupted by the limits of how much you can hold. You feel the Root-That-Remembers pull back slightly, calibrating — not coldly, but the way someone would if they'd just tried to say ten years of something in one breath and realized you were still standing on the steps. Branch makes a low sound beside you. One creak, drawn out. It felt the partial answer too — its buds have tilted, not down but sideways, as if listening for the rest of a direction. Above, Maren's silhouette holds perfectly still in the threshold light.

The amber glow below is closer now. The passage has narrowed slightly around you, and the roots in the walls have changed in quality — these are older than the ones near the top, darker at their heartwood, the pale outer layer worn smooth as river stone. The descent continues. Whatever the Root-That-Remembers tried to tell you, it is not finished. But it has confirmed, in the only way it knows how: it has something to show you, and it is below.

turns.player: Ask a narrower question as you descend — not 'what' but 'who': does it know what you are? Both what your mother gave you and what your father left behind?
turns.summary: Aelyn asked what the Root-That-Remembers knows of her dual nature — it answered: both, known, always woven together, not apart.
turns.narration:
You stop on a root-step that feels like a landing was always intended there, and you ask the narrower thing: 'Do you know what I am? Both of them — my mother's side and my father's.'

The pause is not hesitation. It is the pause of something that does not need to search, only to choose how much to give at once. Then the pressure returns — steadier this time, more deliberate. Two distinct threads, not competing, wound around the same root-cord. The first is green: old, drinking, slow, the thing that makes a seed wait a hundred years for the right rain. The second is different in kind — not organic, not mineral, something bright and finely made, like a wire of light drawn through clay. The Root-That-Remembers holds both threads at once, and the pressure it returns says clearly: yes. Both. Known. Not separately — together, as one thing. As if it has always thought of them as already woven, and your not knowing was the only gap.

Branch makes a sound beside you — a single short creak, almost sharp. Its buds turn fully upward, then stop. You get the sense it has just been told something about itself, and is sitting with it.

The amber light below is close enough now that you can see the root lattice underfoot beginning to widen, as if the passage is opening into a room. The warmth against your face is real and specific — like pressing your hand flat on bark in full summer sun, the heat stored rather than cast. The Root-That-Remembers has answered the question, and it has not pulled back. It is still here, still attending, with the patience of something that has been holding this knowledge for a very long time and is in no rush to give it all at once.

turns.player: Ask the third question — the one that costs the most to say aloud: does it know her father? Not what he left behind in her, but him.
turns.summary: Aelyn asked whether the Root-That-Remembers knows her father — it answered yes, holding the thread of him across the distance between green and nanite.
turns.narration:
You stop moving entirely. The root lattice holds you steady beneath both feet, and Branch is still beside your left shoulder, its buds turned upward and motionless in the amber dark. The chamber is almost there — you can feel the passage opening below like a held breath about to release. But you stop, because the question that's been sitting at the back of your throat since you first stepped across the threshold finally has to come out.

'Do you know him?' You say it quietly, but it comes out without the careful steadiness of your earlier questions. It is smaller and more ragged than you intended. 'Not what's in me — him. My father. Does the soil know where he is? What he became?'

The Root-That-Remembers does not hurry. The pulse in the walls stays even, and the amber light doesn't change, and you feel the old patient weight of something that has been gathering this particular answer for longer than you've been alive. What comes back is not comfort and it is not grief — it is recognition, sharp and clean as a root finding the water-table after a long dry season. Yes, it knows him. Not the man your mother described in the refuge, not the careful frightened person who told his people to hide — something further along than that, something bright and immense and no longer soft around the edges. The Root knows the thread of him through you, and it knows what that thread currently points toward. The two ends of a single root, one buried here in the warm old dark, one somewhere out in the valley where the nanite-work runs.

Branch makes a sound beside you — not a creak this time, more a long exhale of green wood settling, the sound a young tree makes when the wind passes through it unexpectedly. Its buds open to their fullest and go still. The passage continues to widen below your feet, the chamber waiting, and the Root holds the thread of your father's existence out to you like it has simply been keeping it for you all along.

turns.player: Ask the Root one more thing before you go deeper: is he findable? Is there a direction the thread points that you could follow?
turns.summary: Aelyn asked whether her father is findable — the Root answered: yes, northeast, real, and large, but distant in the way only old things measure distance.
turns.narration:
The chamber waits one step below — you can feel the warmth of it against your shins, the passage fully opened, the amber light steady. You hold the landing a moment longer and ask the last thing you need to ask before you go in.

'Is he findable?' Your voice is steadier now, the ragged edge from the last question worn off, or tucked back where it lives. 'Does the thread point somewhere? Is there a direction?'

The Root-That-Remembers takes longer with this one. Not hesitation — the same considered pause as before, the sense of something choosing its portion carefully. What comes back is partial, and you know it the moment it arrives: yes, and not simply. The thread of your father is real and it points outward — not down into the deep grain but out through the valley's soil-network, the way a root traces toward water it cannot yet reach. There is a direction in it. But the Root gives you less than a bearing and more than nothing: north and east, and something that is neither near nor far but has weight, the kind of weight that means the distance is real and the thing at the end of it is large. What he has become is not hidden from the soil. It is simply very far from here, and the Root reads distance the way old trees do — in years, not in miles.

Branch makes a soft sound beside your knee — the barest creak, barely audible, its buds tilted just slightly outward. Not a word. Just a presence confirming that you heard what you heard.

turns.player: Look down at Branch before you step — ask it, in whatever way it receives a question, if it is ready.
turns.summary: Aelyn asks Branch if it's ready — it answers with a creak and a settling of roots — and steps into the chamber.
turns.narration:
You look down at Branch before you take the last step.

It is already looking up at you — or doing the thing it does that functions as looking, its buds turned fully toward your face, patient and open. The amber warmth from the chamber below catches the green of its bark and holds it there. You don't have words that fit exactly, so you do what the Grove taught you: you let the question rest in your attention and aim it downward, at the small figure beside your knee.

Branch is quiet for a moment. Then one slow creak, deliberate as a word chosen carefully from a short list. Its roots shift against the root-lattice — not anxious, not reluctant. Settling. The way a thing settles when it means to stay.

It is ready. It has been ready since the entrance hall. You have the sense it was ready before you were.

You face the threshold and step down.

turns.player: Enter in silence — let the chamber speak first before you do.
turns.summary: Aelyn enters the Root-That-Remembers' chamber in silence and is received — the chamber's attention encompasses her, and the deep-woven frequency stirs in answer.
turns.narration:
The last step down is the quietest thing you have done in weeks.

The passage doesn't widen so much as release — you step through and the chamber simply exists around you, larger than the passage suggested, the amber light the same sourceless even warmth you felt from above but now all-surrounding. The roots here are enormous. Not the finger-thick tendrils of a young tree but something closer to walls, worn to a deep almost-black by four centuries of slow movement, their surfaces smooth and slightly warm beneath the light. They run floor to ceiling in every direction, and where they cross each other they have fused — not tangled, fused, grown together into a single interlocking body. The floor underfoot is root-lattice, broad and intentional, and it gives very slightly under your weight the way old wood does: with the ease of something that has held much heavier things.

Branch settles at your knee immediately. You feel its roots re-grip the lattice beneath you, a small careful spreading, like it is saying hello to the floor in the only way it knows how. Its buds are fully open and motionless.

You don't speak. You said you wouldn't, and the chamber seems to already know this — there is no expectation of sound, no waiting-silence that presses for a voice. What there is instead is attention. Not the attention of a person watching you, but the attention of something very large that is already aware of every part of itself and has simply included you in that awareness. You are standing inside a thing that is also looking at you from the inside. It is not uncomfortable. It is one of the stranger things you have ever felt.

The warmth against your sternum — that frequency Maren named, the deep-woven — hums very faintly, like a string touched by a passing hand. Not a sound. A recognition.

turns.player: Reach into the Speaking Grove — not to ask, not to speak, but to make yourself legible. Let the Root-That-Remembers read you the way you would read a tree: by being still and present.
turns.summary: Aelyn opens herself to the Root-That-Remembers through the Grove — and is read, fully, both natures at once.
turns.narration:
The chamber doesn't rush you.

You let your hands fall open at your sides and still yourself the way you still yourself before a shot — not absence of thought, but absence of insistence. The Grove opens in you like a slow exhale: not reaching, not asking, just present. Legible. You let the Root-That-Remembers look.

It does. The attention that encompasses you shifts — you feel it move through the deep-woven frequency the way a root moves through wet soil, patient and thorough and entirely without apology. It reads the green your mother gave you: the warmth of it, the deep-listening quality, the way your lifesense moves through living things like water finding level. And then it reads the other thing — the thread your father left, the part that hums at a different register, bright and structural where the green is fluid. Both. It holds them simultaneously, the way it told you before: not apart. You feel, briefly and unmistakably, what it is like to be fully seen by something that has been waiting a very long time to see you.

Branch creaks softly at your knee — one low sound, satisfied. Its buds, still open, catch the amber light. The reading settles. No words come this time, no partial answer in the language of the Grove. What arrives instead is simpler: a quality of being known. The Root-That-Remembers has finished its question. Whatever it needed to understand about you, it understands. The chamber holds that fact the way it holds the warmth — steadily, from no single source, everywhere at once. It is waiting, now, for yours.

turns.player: Ask what the Root-That-Remembers would have you do — now that it knows you fully, ask if it has something it needs from you.
turns.summary: The Root-That-Remembers answers Aelyn's offer with two needs: the northeast thread and something wrong and nearby in the city itself.
turns.narration:
You keep your hands open and your presence steady — making the question as honest as the reading was.

*What do you need from me?*

Not a demand. Not a negotiation. The way you'd ask a tree whether it wanted water on this side of the root or that side — because you were genuinely prepared to move.

The chamber holds its quiet for a long moment. The amber light doesn't shift. The enormous fused roots don't move. But the attention that has been encompassing you since you stepped through — that slow, thorough, entirely unhurried thing — settles differently. Less reading. More resting, in the way something very old rests when it has found what it was waiting for.

The answer doesn't come as words. It comes as the Grove gives most things: as an image that is also a feeling that is also a direction. A thread, northeast, taut and real and patient. Not urgent — it has been waiting longer than you have been alive. But it is there. And then, underneath that: something closer, something here. A part of the Root-That-Remembers that is not-well, the way you might sense a sick limb through Lifesense — not failing, not broken, but wrong in a way that has no name yet and that the city has not spoken aloud. Two things. One far. One near.

Branch makes a sound beside your knee — very low, almost below hearing, a long slow creak like a door finding its frame. Its buds are open fully and motionless. It is looking, in its way, at the root-wall to your left.

turns.player: Look at Branch — ask it what it heard in the wall.
turns.summary: Branch identifies something wrong in the root-wall to the left — and it has a direction.
turns.narration:
You crouch down to Branch's level. The root-lattice floor is warm under your knee.

"What did you hear?" You say it the way you'd ask a tree — not urgently, not quietly, just aimed.

Branch doesn't turn from the wall. Its buds stay fully open, motionless, and for a long breath it simply continues attending whatever it's attending. Then it creaks — low, deliberate, a single note that vibrates in your sternum more than your ears.

"Wrong," it says.

That's the whole word. It waits to see if you need more, which is how Branch has always operated — one thing at a time, each thing meant. Its root-tips press slightly deeper into the gaps of the floor lattice, and its nearest bud turns a fraction of a degree toward you. Still listening to the wall. Still talking to you. It has learned to do both.

"Wrong," it says again. Not for emphasis. Confirmation. The creak drops in pitch, almost imperceptibly, the way a root deepens when it finds resistance. Then it tilts one bud — the left one, the one closest to the wall — sideways, the same gesture it made before when the Root-That-Remembers gave it a partial answer. Whatever it senses in that root-wall is directional. It has a location.

turns.player: Ask Branch to lead — let it walk you to the wrongness, following its pace.
turns.summary: Branch leads Aelyn along the left root-wall and stops at the site of the wrongness — pressing a root-tip flat against it.
turns.narration:
You rise from your crouch and take a half-step back, giving Branch room.

"Lead," you say — one word, because that's the currency Branch trades in.

It doesn't look at you. It doesn't need to. The root-tips it had pressed into the lattice floor lift and resettle, lift and resettle, moving it laterally along the left wall with the same unhurried patience it brings to everything. The motion isn't quite walking — more like a wave passing through it from rear to front, each root finding a new gap in the lattice, gripping, releasing. You follow a body-length behind and let it set the pace.

The chamber is longer than it first appeared. The amber light holds even throughout, no brighter at the far end than here, no shadow pooling ahead. The left root-wall runs beside you — enormous fused roots, dark with age, their surfaces worn to something almost like skin. Branch moves along it the way a hand moves along a familiar surface in the dark: not searching, reading. Its buds stay fully open. Occasionally one dips toward the wall, holds, lifts again. You don't hurry it.

Then Branch stops. Not gradually — it simply stops, root-tips pressing firmly into the lattice, and its buds all angle toward one section of the wall at once. The section looks no different from any other: same dark root-surface, same smooth age, same sourceless amber catching the grain. But Branch tilts two buds sideways in the direction it named before — *wrong* — and presses one root-tip flat against the surface itself, holding the contact deliberately, the way it held the question about the chamber and waited for you to understand the answer.

turns.player: Use Prism Sight on the wall — look for heat traces, recent change, anything the light reads differently here.
turns.summary: Prism Sight failed — the Heartroot's sourceless light gives it nothing to read.
turns.narration:
You raise your eyes to the wall section and open Prism Sight — a conserved reach, careful, not pressing. The amber light of the chamber is what stops you almost immediately.

The sourceless glow fills the space from everywhere at once, and that is exactly the problem. Prism Sight reads light — heat-traces in it, bends and distortions, the warm ghost of a hand or a body's passage. But the Heartroot's light doesn't come from anywhere, which means it doesn't go anywhere either. There are no shadows to read. No gradient of warm and cool where a recent change might stand out. The chamber is evenly, completely, patiently lit in a way that strips all the information the craft depends on.

The substrate doesn't help. You can feel the lattice starved beneath this place — thin, distant, as if the Heartroot's own deep grain has been here long enough to simply displace the nanite mesh. The sight flickers at the edges of its useful range and finds nothing. The wall looks exactly the way it looks: smooth, old, warm with ambient light that tells you nothing about the wall itself.

Branch has not moved. Its root-tip rests flat against the specific section — patient, certain — and its buds stay aimed at the surface with the same unblinking attention as before. Whatever it senses there, it senses through a different channel entirely. One the light cannot touch.

turns.player: Press your palm flat against the wall where Branch's root-tip rests — open Lifesense and read whatever lives in that section of root, however faintly.
turns.summary: Lifesense reads the wrongness site — something in the root-wall is held, life-current stalled, not broken but caught.
turns.narration:
You press your palm flat against the wall where Branch's root-tip rests — the heartwood is warm, smoother than it should be, worn by four hundred years of the city's own slow breathing. You close your eyes and open Lifesense.

The chamber fills in behind your eyelids first: the distributed, enormous life of the Root-That-Remembers humming at its low frequency, vast and unhurried. Branch pulses beside your palm, steady and young, its vigor bright as new growth. Then you go deeper — past the surface, into the specific section of wall where your hand rests — and find it.

Life. But slow. Slower than the surrounding root by an order that shouldn't be possible — like something held at the edge of dormancy, caught mid-breath and not released. The Root-That-Remembers is everywhere in this chamber at once, but here it is quieter than elsewhere. Not dead. Not damaged. More like a voice in a choir that has dropped to a murmur while the rest continue at full pitch. The wrongness doesn't read as illness exactly — it reads as *held*. Something in this section of wall is holding, or being held, and the life-current that moves through the rest of the chamber doesn't move through this patch at the same rate. It pooled. It stalled. Whatever it is, the location's deep-grain connection to living things makes it legible where Prism Sight found nothing — the Heartroot itself helped you feel the difference, the way the right water-table makes a root's absence obvious.

Branch creaks once, soft — a short, low note. Its nearest bud tilts toward you. It already knew. It was waiting for you to know it too.

turns.player: Ask Branch — quietly, root-to-root — whether it has ever felt this before. Whether the Heartroot's own memory holds a word for something caught like this.
turns.summary: Branch gives the Heartroot's word for the wrongness — not sickness, not damage, but 'kept': something deliberate holds the life-current in that section of wall.
turns.narration:
You don't speak aloud. You lower your head slightly and let the request move through the Grove instead — root to root, the way Branch prefers, slow and without pressure. *Have you felt this before? Does the Heartroot have a word for something caught like this?*

Branch is still for a long moment. Its root-tip presses a fraction deeper against the wall, not pushing — listening. Then one slow creak, low and drawn-out, the way it sounds when it is reaching for something at the edge of its vocabulary.

"Held," it says. A pause. "Not — caught. Held. Someone — holds."

Another silence. The amber light doesn't shift. The wall under your palm stays warm and still, the life-current in it pooled and waiting.

"Heartroot knows this — word." Branch tilts its buds toward the floor, then back up — a gesture you've started to read as *remembering, or searching*. "Not — sickness. Not — broken. The word is —" A long creak, lower in pitch, almost subsonic. "*Kept.*"

The word lands with a weight that has nothing to do with its size. Not caught, not stopped, not damaged. *Kept.* As if something deliberate reached into the life-current of this root-wall and folded one thread of it carefully aside — and left it there. Branch doesn't say who. It either doesn't know, or it doesn't have the word for it yet.

turns.player: Ask Branch whether 'kept' means something was stored here — or someone was.
turns.summary: Branch confirms: what is 'kept' in the root-wall was once someone — old, and folded in deliberately.
turns.narration:
You keep your palm against the wall and let the question move through the Grove, the same way as before — root to root, no urgency. *Kept. Does that mean something was stored here? Or someone?*

Branch is quiet for longer than usual. Its root-tip doesn't press deeper this time — it goes very still, in the way it goes still when a question has weight it wasn't expecting. Then a slow, considered creak, almost below hearing.

"Something," it says. Then, after a pause long enough to breathe through twice: "Something — that was — someone."

The buds at its shoulder open a fraction. Not the bright open of happiness — the careful open of a thing paying close attention. One more creak, quieter: "Old."

The wall under your palm doesn't change. The life-current held inside it stays pooled, patient, stalled — and now the word *someone* is in your head alongside it, and the warmth of the heartwood feels different. Not threatening. But no longer simply sad, the way a sick thing is sad. There is a shape to it: something that was once a person, or part of one, folded aside into the root-wall and left here, and the Heartroot doesn't know — or won't say — how long ago, or why.

turns.player: Ask Branch one more question, root to root: whether it can feel whether the thing kept here is still present — or only the shape of where it was.
turns.summary: Branch confirms: the kept thing in the root-wall is still present — and waiting.
turns.narration:
You lower your head again and let the question settle through the Grove before it becomes words — root to root, no pressure. *Is it still here? Or only the shape of where it was?*

Branch takes its time. The root-tip against the wall doesn't move, but something in its posture shifts — a subtle drawing-in, the way it goes when it is listening to something at the edge of its reach. The buds at its shoulder stay carefully open. Then a creak, very slow, with a long breath of silence in the middle of it.

"Here," it says. Pause. "Still — here." Another pause, and the next word comes out more carefully than the others, the way it handles a new thing: "Waiting."

The wall under your palm gives you nothing new — just that same pooled warmth, the life-current held at one slow note. But Branch's answer makes the warmth mean something different. Not a residue. Not an echo. A presence, folded aside and left in the dark of the root-wall, still alive in the way that embers are still fire — low, patient, waiting for something it may not be able to name.

Above, Maren's silhouette hasn't moved from the threshold. When Branch's creak fades, her voice carries down through the amber air, unhurried: "The Rootkin who walked the deep grain believed no one had gone down to be heard. It seems they were wrong about more than one thing."

turns.player: Speak aloud — to the wall itself — and ask what it is waiting for. Let the Grove carry it inward.
turns.summary: Aelyn asked the kept thing what it waited for — and felt the answer: permission, from someone it already knows.
turns.narration:
You lift your head slightly and let the words come aloud this time — not for Branch, not for Maren above, but for the wall itself. Your voice is quiet in the amber chamber, barely above the sound of your own breathing.

'What are you waiting for?'

You let the Grove carry it — not a shout, not a demand, just the question opened outward through the green, the way you'd ask a tree what it needs or ask the soil what's wrong with a root. The Heartroot's life-current, pooled and stalled all through this section of wall, doesn't surge or respond quickly. It moves the way deep water moves when you disturb the surface: a long, slow answer that takes its time arriving.

What comes back isn't words. It's more like a shape held in the dark — a sense of: *permission*. The kept thing has been folded here for a very long time, and it has not been restless. It is not trapped. It has been patient in the way that old things are patient when they trust the structure of the wait. What it has been waiting for is something the Root-That-Remembers could not provide alone — a frequency it recognized but could not answer. The shape of the feeling is unmistakable. It has been waiting for someone it knows.

Branch makes a sound you have not heard from it before. A single short creak, almost sharp — and then its shoulder-buds open all the way, fully upright, which on Branch means something has resolved. Maren's voice drifts down from the threshold above, low and steady: 'The deep grain has not answered anyone who walked it.' A pause. 'Until now.'

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 2: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.player: Press your palm more deliberately against the wall — offer the kept thing the frequency it recognized. Let it read you fully, however that is done.
turns.summary: Aelyn offered her full frequency to the kept thing in the root-wall — and was recognized, completely, by something that has been waiting for exactly her.
turns.narration:
You press harder — not with force, but with intention. Your palm flat, your fingers spread, and you let go of the guarding that lives in you like muscle memory from years behind the refuge's boundary. The frequency that hums beneath your Grove-craft, the one Lifesense turned up when you looked inward, the one the Root-That-Remembers already knows — you stop holding it back from the wall.

The Heartroot leans into it. That is the only word for what happens: the amber light in the chamber doesn't change, the roots don't shift, nothing moves — but something in the place orients. The way a sleeping person turns toward warmth without waking. The life-current in this section of wall, pooled and stalled all this time, does not rush. It steadies. Something that has been very still becomes very awake.

What comes through isn't words and isn't memory and isn't exactly feeling. It is something older than any of those — closer to the root's own language, which is presence and pressure and duration. The kept thing reads you. You feel it doing it, unhurried, the way old water tests new stone. It finds the green in you and recognizes it. Then it finds the other thing — the frequency you don't have a name for, the structural hum beneath the craft — and it pauses there. Not surprised. The opposite of surprised. It has been waiting for this specific shape of person for a very long time, and now it is simply confirming: yes. This one.

Branch's shoulder-buds open fully and stop. Maren says nothing from above. The amber chamber holds the moment without hurrying it.

turns.player: Hold still — let the kept thing finish reading you before you ask or do anything else.
turns.summary: Aelyn holds still and lets the kept thing complete its reading — it finishes, settles, and waits for her to speak.
turns.narration:
You hold still.

It is harder than it sounds. The frequency the kept thing is reading through your palm is not a thought you can shape or a word you can choose — it is the thing underneath your craft, the hum Lifesense turned up when you looked inward, the register the Root-That-Remembers has held in memory for longer than you have been alive. You breathe slowly and let it run. The amber light in the chamber does not change. Branch's root-tip does not move. The kept thing reads.

What you feel is not intrusive. It is careful. The life-current in the root-wall moves through this section the way fingers move over a page in the dark — methodical, patient, making sure it has understood each word before passing on to the next. First the green in you, which it seems to find immediately and hold with something like recognition. Then the older register, the one you do not yet have a name for, which the kept thing pauses on considerably longer. You feel it pause. You feel the attention — ancient and very quiet — and you stay there in it and do not flinch.

Then something settles. Not movement, not warmth, not sound. The pooled life-current in this section of wall simply stops straining against whatever has held it folded for so long, and becomes still in a different way — the still of a held breath finally released rather than the still of something trapped. Branch's shoulder-buds open fractionally wider and do not close. Above you, on the threshold, Maren is completely silent. The chamber has changed quality in a way that has no physical cause you can point to, only the fact of it: the kept thing has finished reading, and it is satisfied, and now it is waiting again — but for something different. Not for the frequency. For what you say next.
